steps.title: Read the histogram and identify the data distribution
steps.content:
From the histogram, the 14 salmon weights are distributed as:
  • [70, 75): 2 salmon
  • [75, 80): 1 salmon
  • [80, 85): 2 salmon
  • [85, 90): 6 salmon
  • [90, 95): 3 salmon
The new weight of 32 pounds is much smaller than all original weights (minimum 70 pounds), so it becomes the new minimum when added.

steps.title: Analyze the median comparison
steps.content:
For 14 original values, the median is $\frac{v_7 + v_8}{2}$ (average of positions 7 and 8).

When 32 is added as the new minimum, all original values shift up by one position. The new 15-value dataset has median = 8th value = $v_7$ (the original 7th value).

For the new median to be less, we need $v_7 < \frac{v_7 + v_8}{2}$, which requires $v_7 < v_8$. Checking cumulative positions: positions 1–2 are from [70, 75), position 3 from [75, 80), positions 4–5 from [80, 85), and positions 6–11 from [85, 90). Both $v_7$ and $v_8$ lie in [85, 90). Since they come from the same interval, we cannot guarantee $v_7 < v_8$. The median does not necessarily decrease.

steps.title: Analyze the mean comparison
steps.content:
Let $S$ = sum of the original 14 weights. The original mean is $\frac{S}{14}$ and the new mean is $\frac{S + 32}{15}$.

For the new mean to be less: $$\frac{S + 32}{15} < \frac{S}{14}$$ Cross-multiplying gives $14(S + 32) < 15S$, which simplifies to $14S + 448 < 15S$, or $S > 448$.

From the histogram, all weights are at least 70 pounds, so $S \geq 14(70) = 980 > 448$. The inequality holds, so the mean MUST decrease. That matches choice B — B is correct.
